modules.name: audio
modules.description: Provides an interface to create noise with the user's speakers.

modules.name: data
modules.description: Provides functionality for creating and transforming data.

modules.name: filesystem
modules.description: Provides an interface to the user's filesystem.

modules.name: font
modules.description: Allows you to work with fonts.

modules.name: graphics
modules.description:
The primary responsibility for the love.graphics module is the drawing of lines, shapes, text, Images and other Drawable objects onto the screen. Its secondary responsibilities include loading external files (including Images and Fonts) into memory, creating specialized objects (such as ParticleSystems or Canvases) and managing screen geometry.

LÖVE's coordinate system is rooted in the upper-left corner of the screen, which is at location (0, 0). The x axis is horizontal: larger values are further to the right. The y axis is vertical: larger values are further towards the bottom.

In many cases, you draw images or shapes in terms of their upper-left corner.

Many of the functions are used to manipulate the graphics coordinate system, which is essentially the way coordinates are mapped to the display. You can change the position, scale, and even rotation in this way.

modules.name: image
modules.description: Provides an interface to decode encoded image data.

modules.name: joystick
modules.description: Provides an interface to the user's joystick.

modules.name: keyboard
modules.description: Provides an interface to the user's keyboard.

modules.name: mouse
modules.description: Provides an interface to the user's mouse.

modules.name: physics
modules.description: Can simulate 2D rigid body physics in a realistic manner. This module is based on Box2D, and this API corresponds to the Box2D API as closely as possible.

modules.name: sound
modules.description: This module is responsible for decoding sound files. It can't play the sounds, see love.audio for that.

modules.name: system
modules.description: Provides access to information about the user's system.

modules.name: thread
modules.description:
Allows you to work with threads.

Threads are separate Lua environments, running in parallel to the main code. As their code runs separately, they can be used to compute complex operations without adversely affecting the frame rate of the main thread. However, as they are separate environments, they cannot access the variables and functions of the main thread, and communication between threads is limited.

All LOVE objects (userdata) are shared among threads so you'll only have to send their references across threads. You may run into concurrency issues if you manipulate an object on multiple threads at the same time.

When a Thread is started, it only loads the love.thread module. Every other module has to be loaded with require.

modules.name: timer
modules.description: Provides an interface to the user's clock.

modules.name: touch
modules.description: Provides an interface to touch-screen presses.

modules.name: video
modules.description:
This module is responsible for decoding, controlling, and streaming video files.

It can't draw the videos, see love.graphics.newVideo and Video objects for that.

modules.name: window
modules.description: Provides an interface for modifying and retrieving information about the program's window.

symbols.name: getRecordingDevices
symbols.description:
Gets a list of RecordingDevices on the system.

The first device in the list is the user's default recording device. The list may be empty if there are no microphones connected to the system.

Audio recording is currently not supported on iOS.

returns.name: devices
returns.description: The list of connected recording devices.

symbols.name: newQueueableSource
symbols.description: Creates a new Source usable for real-time generated sound playback with Source:queue.

arguments.name: samplerate
arguments.description: Number of samples per second when playing.

arguments.name: bitdepth
arguments.description: Bits per sample (8 or 16).

arguments.name: channels
arguments.description: 1 for mono or 2 for stereo.

arguments.name: buffercount
arguments.description: The number of buffers that can be queued up at any given time with Source:queue. Cannot be greater than 64. A sensible default (~8) is chosen if no value is specified.

returns.name: source
returns.description: The new Source usable with Source:queue.

symbols.name: newSource
symbols.description:
Creates a new Source from a filepath, File, Decoder or SoundData.

Sources created from SoundData are always static.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filepath to the audio file.

arguments.name: type
arguments.description: Streaming or static source.

returns.name: source
returns.description: A new Source that can play the specified audio.

arguments.name: file
arguments.description: A File pointing to an audio file.

arguments.name: type
arguments.description: Streaming or static source.

returns.name: source
returns.description: A new Source that can play the specified audio.

arguments.name: decoder
arguments.description: The Decoder to create a Source from.

arguments.name: type
arguments.description: Streaming or static source.

returns.name: source
returns.description: A new Source that can play the specified audio.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The FileData to create a Source from.

arguments.name: type
arguments.description: Streaming or static source.

returns.name: source
returns.description: A new Source that can play the specified audio.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The SoundData to create a Source from.

returns.name: source
returns.description: A new Source that can play the specified audio. The SourceType of the returned audio is 'static'.

symbols.name: getBitDepth
symbols.description: Gets the number of bits per sample in the data currently being recorded.

returns.name: bits
returns.description: The number of bits per sample in the data that's currently being recorded.

symbols.name: getChannelCount
symbols.description: Gets the number of channels currently being recorded (mono or stereo).

returns.name: channels
returns.description: The number of channels being recorded (1 for mono, 2 for stereo).

symbols.name: getData
symbols.description:
Gets all recorded audio SoundData stored in the device's internal ring buffer.

The internal ring buffer is cleared when this function is called, so calling it again will only get audio recorded after the previous call. If the device's internal ring buffer completely fills up before getData is called, the oldest data that doesn't fit into the buffer will be lost.

returns.name: data
returns.description: The recorded audio data, or nil if the device isn't recording.

symbols.name: getName
symbols.description: Gets the name of the recording device.

returns.name: name
returns.description: The name of the device.

symbols.name: getSampleCount
symbols.description: Gets the number of currently recorded samples.

returns.name: samples
returns.description: The number of samples that have been recorded so far.

symbols.name: getSampleRate
symbols.description: Gets the number of samples per second currently being recorded.

returns.name: rate
returns.description: The number of samples being recorded per second (sample rate).

symbols.name: isRecording
symbols.description: Gets whether the device is currently recording.

returns.name: recording
returns.description: True if the recording, false otherwise.

symbols.name: start
symbols.description: Begins recording audio using this device.

arguments.name: samplecount
arguments.description: The maximum number of samples to store in an internal ring buffer when recording. RecordingDevice:getData clears the internal buffer when called.

arguments.name: samplerate
arguments.description: The number of samples per second to store when recording.

arguments.name: bitdepth
arguments.description: The number of bits per sample.

arguments.name: channels
arguments.description: Whether to record in mono or stereo. Most microphones don't support more than 1 channel.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the device successfully began recording using the specified parameters, false if not.

symbols.name: stop
symbols.description: Stops recording audio from this device. Any sound data currently in the device's buffer will be returned.

returns.name: data
returns.description: The sound data currently in the device's buffer, or nil if the device wasn't recording.

symbols.name: clone
symbols.description:
Creates an identical copy of the Source in the stopped state.

Static Sources will use significantly less memory and take much less time to be created if Source:clone is used to create them instead of love.audio.newSource, so this method should be preferred when making multiple Sources which play the same sound.

returns.name: source
returns.description: The new identical copy of this Source.

symbols.name: getActiveEffects
symbols.description: Gets a list of the Source's active effect names.

returns.name: effects
returns.description: A list of the source's active effect names.

symbols.name: getAirAbsorption
symbols.description:
Gets the amount of air absorption applied to the Source.

By default the value is set to 0 which means that air absorption effects are disabled. A value of 1 will apply high frequency attenuation to the Source at a rate of 0.05 dB per meter.

returns.name: amount
returns.description: The amount of air absorption applied to the Source.

symbols.name: getAttenuationDistances
symbols.description: Gets the reference and maximum attenuation distances of the Source. The values, combined with the current DistanceModel, affect how the Source's volume attenuates based on distance from the listener.

returns.name: ref
returns.description: The current reference attenuation distance. If the current DistanceModel is clamped, this is the minimum distance before the Source is no longer attenuated.

returns.name: max
returns.description: The current maximum attenuation distance.

symbols.name: getChannelCount
symbols.description: Gets the number of channels in the Source. Only 1-channel (mono) Sources can use directional and positional effects.

returns.name: channels
returns.description: 1 for mono, 2 for stereo.

symbols.name: getCone
symbols.description: Gets the Source's directional volume cones. Together with Source:setDirection, the cone angles allow for the Source's volume to vary depending on its direction.

returns.name: innerAngle
returns.description: The inner angle from the Source's direction, in radians. The Source will play at normal volume if the listener is inside the cone defined by this angle.

returns.name: outerAngle
returns.description: The outer angle from the Source's direction, in radians. The Source will play at a volume between the normal and outer volumes, if the listener is in between the cones defined by the inner and outer angles.

returns.name: outerVolume
returns.description: The Source's volume when the listener is outside both the inner and outer cone angles.

symbols.name: getDirection
symbols.description: Gets the direction of the Source.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The X part of the direction vector.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The Y part of the direction vector.

returns.name: z
returns.description: The Z part of the direction vector.

symbols.name: getDuration
symbols.description: Gets the duration of the Source. For streaming Sources it may not always be sample-accurate, and may return -1 if the duration cannot be determined at all.

arguments.name: unit
arguments.description: The time unit for the return value.

returns.name: duration
returns.description: The duration of the Source, or -1 if it cannot be determined.

symbols.name: getEffect
symbols.description:
Gets the filter settings associated to a specific effect.

This function returns nil if the effect was applied with no filter settings associated to it.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The name of the effect.

arguments.name: filtersettings
arguments.description: An optional empty table that will be filled with the filter settings.

returns.name: filtersettings
returns.description: The settings for the filter associated to this effect, or nil if the effect is not present in this Source or has no filter associated. The table has the following fields:

symbols.name: getFilter
symbols.description: Gets the filter settings currently applied to the Source.

returns.name: settings
returns.description: The filter settings to use for this Source, or nil if the Source has no active filter. The table has the following fields:

symbols.name: getFreeBufferCount
symbols.description: Gets the number of free buffer slots in a queueable Source. If the queueable Source is playing, this value will increase up to the amount the Source was created with. If the queueable Source is stopped, it will process all of its internal buffers first, in which case this function will always return the amount it was created with.

returns.name: buffers
returns.description: How many more SoundData objects can be queued up.

symbols.name: pause
symbols.description: Pauses the Source.

symbols.description: Starts playing the Source.

returns.name: success

symbols.name: queue
symbols.description:
Queues SoundData for playback in a queueable Source.

This method requires the Source to be created via love.audio.newQueueableSource.

arguments.name: sounddata
arguments.description: The data to queue. The SoundData's sample rate, bit depth, and channel count must match the Source's.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the data was successfully queued for playback, false if there were no available buffers to use for queueing.

symbols.name: seek
symbols.description: Sets the currently playing position of the Source.

arguments.name: offset
arguments.description: The position to seek to.

arguments.name: unit
arguments.description: The unit of the position value.

symbols.name: setAirAbsorption
symbols.description:
Sets the amount of air absorption applied to the Source.

By default the value is set to 0 which means that air absorption effects are disabled. A value of 1 will apply high frequency attenuation to the Source at a rate of 0.05 dB per meter.

Air absorption can simulate sound transmission through foggy air, dry air, smoky atmosphere, etc. It can be used to simulate different atmospheric conditions within different locations in an area.

arguments.name: amount
arguments.description: The amount of air absorption applied to the Source. Must be between 0 and 10.

symbols.name: setAttenuationDistances
symbols.description:
Sets the reference and maximum attenuation distances of the Source. The parameters, combined with the current DistanceModel, affect how the Source's volume attenuates based on distance.

Distance attenuation is only applicable to Sources based on mono (rather than stereo) audio.

arguments.name: ref
arguments.description: The new reference attenuation distance. If the current DistanceModel is clamped, this is the minimum attenuation distance.

arguments.name: max
arguments.description: The new maximum attenuation distance.

symbols.name: setCone
symbols.description: Sets the Source's directional volume cones. Together with Source:setDirection, the cone angles allow for the Source's volume to vary depending on its direction.

arguments.name: innerAngle
arguments.description: The inner angle from the Source's direction, in radians. The Source will play at normal volume if the listener is inside the cone defined by this angle.

arguments.name: outerAngle
arguments.description: The outer angle from the Source's direction, in radians. The Source will play at a volume between the normal and outer volumes, if the listener is in between the cones defined by the inner and outer angles.

arguments.name: outerVolume
arguments.description: The Source's volume when the listener is outside both the inner and outer cone angles.

symbols.name: setDirection
symbols.description: Sets the direction vector of the Source. A zero vector makes the source non-directional.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The X part of the direction vector.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The Y part of the direction vector.

arguments.name: z
arguments.description: The Z part of the direction vector.

symbols.name: setEffect
symbols.description:
Applies an audio effect to the Source.

The effect must have been previously defined using love.audio.setEffect.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The name of the effect previously set up with love.audio.setEffect.

arguments.name: enable
arguments.description: If false and the given effect name was previously enabled on this Source, disables the effect.

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the effect was successfully applied to this Source.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The name of the effect previously set up with love.audio.setEffect.

arguments.name: filtersettings
arguments.description: The filter settings to apply prior to the effect, with the following fields:

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the effect and filter were successfully applied to this Source.

symbols.name: setFilter
symbols.description: Sets a low-pass, high-pass, or band-pass filter to apply when playing the Source.

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: The filter settings to use for this Source, with the following fields:

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the filter was successfully applied to the Source.

symbols.name: setLooping
symbols.description: Sets whether the Source should loop.

arguments.name: loop
arguments.description: True if the source should loop, false otherwise.

symbols.name: setPitch
symbols.description: Sets the pitch of the Source.

arguments.name: pitch
arguments.description: Calculated with regard to 1 being the base pitch. Each reduction by 50 percent equals a pitch shift of -12 semitones (one octave reduction). Each doubling equals a pitch shift of 12 semitones (one octave increase). Zero is not a legal value.

symbols.name: setPosition
symbols.description: Sets the position of the Source. Please note that this only works for mono (i.e. non-stereo) sound files!

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The X position of the Source.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The Y position of the Source.

arguments.name: z
arguments.description: The Z position of the Source.

symbols.name: setRelative
symbols.description:
Sets whether the Source's position, velocity, direction, and cone angles are relative to the listener, or absolute.

By default, all sources are absolute and therefore relative to the origin of love's coordinate system 0, 0. Only absolute sources are affected by the position of the listener. Please note that positional audio only works for mono (i.e. non-stereo) sources.

arguments.name: enable
arguments.description: True to make the position, velocity, direction and cone angles relative to the listener, false to make them absolute.

symbols.name: setRolloff
symbols.description:
Sets the rolloff factor which affects the strength of the used distance attenuation.

Extended information and detailed formulas can be found in the chapter '3.4. Attenuation By Distance' of OpenAL 1.1 specification.

arguments.name: rolloff
arguments.description: The new rolloff factor.

symbols.name: setVelocity
symbols.description:
Sets the velocity of the Source.

This does '''not''' change the position of the Source, but lets the application know how it has to calculate the doppler effect.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The X part of the velocity vector.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The Y part of the velocity vector.

arguments.name: z
arguments.description: The Z part of the velocity vector.

symbols.name: setVolume
symbols.description: Sets the current volume of the Source.

arguments.name: volume
arguments.description: The volume for a Source, where 1.0 is normal volume. Volume cannot be raised above 1.0.

symbols.name: setVolumeLimits
symbols.description: Sets the volume limits of the source. The limits have to be numbers from 0 to 1.

arguments.name: min
arguments.description: The minimum volume.

arguments.name: max
arguments.description: The maximum volume.

symbols.name: stop
symbols.description: Stops a Source.

symbols.name: tell
symbols.description: Gets the currently playing position of the Source.

arguments.name: unit
arguments.description: The type of unit for the return value.

returns.name: position
returns.description: The currently playing position of the Source.

symbols.name: compress
symbols.description: Compresses a string or data using a specific compression algorithm.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format to use when compressing the string.

arguments.name: rawstring
arguments.description: The raw (un-compressed) string to compress.

arguments.name: level
arguments.description: The level of compression to use, between 0 and 9. -1 indicates the default level. The meaning of this argument depends on the compression format being used.

returns.name: compressedData
returns.description: CompressedData/string which contains the compressed version of rawstring.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the compressed data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format to use when compressing the data.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: A Data object containing the raw (un-compressed) data to compress.

arguments.name: level
arguments.description: The level of compression to use, between 0 and 9. -1 indicates the default level. The meaning of this argument depends on the compression format being used.

returns.name: compressedData
returns.description: CompressedData/string which contains the compressed version of data.

Additional values to serialize.

symbols.name: unpack
symbols.description:
Unpacks (deserializes) a byte-string or Data into simple Lua values.

This function behaves the same as Lua 5.3's string.unpack.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: A string determining how the values were packed. Follows the rules of Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.

arguments.name: datastring
arguments.description: A string containing the packed (serialized) data.

arguments.name: pos
arguments.description: Where to start reading in the string. Negative values can be used to read relative from the end of the string.

returns.name: v1
returns.description: The first value (number, boolean, or string) that was unpacked.
Additional unpacked values.

returns.name: index
returns.description: The index of the first unread byte in the data string.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: A string determining how the values were packed. Follows the rules of Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.

arguments.name: data

arguments.description: 1-based index indicating where to start reading in the Data. Negative values can be used to read relative from the end of the Data object.

returns.name: v1
returns.description: The first value (number, boolean, or string) that was unpacked.
Additional unpacked values.

returns.name: index

symbols.name: clear
symbols.description: Clears the event queue.

symbols.description: Returns an iterator for messages in the event queue.

returns.name: i
returns.description: Iterator function usable in a for loop.

symbols.name: pump
symbols.description:
Pump events into the event queue.

This is a low-level function, and is usually not called by the user, but by love.run.

Note that this does need to be called for any OS to think you're still running,

and if you want to handle OS-generated events at all (think callbacks).

symbols.name: push
symbols.description:
Adds an event to the event queue.

From 0.10.0 onwards, you may pass an arbitrary amount of arguments with this function, though the default callbacks don't ever use more than six.

arguments.name: n
arguments.description: The name of the event.

arguments.name: a
arguments.description: First event argument.

arguments.name: b
arguments.description: Second event argument.

arguments.name: c
arguments.description: Third event argument.

arguments.name: d
arguments.description: Fourth event argument.

arguments.name: e
arguments.description: Fifth event argument.

arguments.name: f
arguments.description: Sixth event argument.
Further event arguments may follow.

symbols.name: quit
symbols.description:
Adds the quit event to the queue.

The quit event is a signal for the event handler to close LÖVE. It's possible to abort the exit process with the love.quit callback.

arguments.name: exitstatus
arguments.description: The program exit status to use when closing the application.

arguments.name: 'restart'
arguments.description: Tells the default love.run to exit and restart the game without relaunching the executable.

symbols.name: wait
symbols.description: Like love.event.poll(), but blocks until there is an event in the queue.

returns.name: n
returns.description: The name of event.

returns.name: a
returns.description: First event argument.

returns.name: b
returns.description: Second event argument.

returns.name: c
returns.description: Third event argument.

returns.name: d
returns.description: Fourth event argument.

returns.name: e
returns.description: Fifth event argument.

returns.name: f
returns.description: Sixth event argument.
Further event arguments may follow.

symbols.name: getAppdataDirectory
symbols.description: Returns the application data directory (could be the same as getUserDirectory)

returns.name: path
returns.description: The path of the application data directory

symbols.name: getCRequirePath
symbols.description:
Gets the filesystem paths that will be searched for c libraries when require is called.

The paths string returned by this function is a sequence of path templates separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory separators.) Additionally, any occurrence of a double question mark ('??') will be replaced by the name passed to require and the default library extension for the platform.

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

returns.name: paths
returns.description: The paths that the ''require'' function will check for c libraries in love's filesystem.

symbols.name: getDirectoryItems
symbols.description:
Returns a table with the names of files and subdirectories in the specified path. The table is not sorted in any way; the order is undefined.

If the path passed to the function exists in the game and the save directory, it will list the files and directories from both places.

arguments.name: dir
arguments.description: The directory.

returns.name: files
returns.description: A sequence with the names of all files and subdirectories as strings.

symbols.name: getIdentity
symbols.description:
Gets the write directory name for your game.

Note that this only returns the name of the folder to store your files in, not the full path.

returns.name: name
returns.description: The identity that is used as write directory.

returns.name: info
returns.description: A table containing information about the specified path, or nil if nothing exists at the path. The table contains the following fields:

arguments.name: path

symbols.name: getRealDirectory

Gets the platform-specific absolute path of the directory containing a filepath.

This can be used to determine whether a file is inside the save directory or the game's source .love.

arguments.name: filepath
arguments.description: The filepath to get the directory of.

returns.name: realdir
returns.description: The platform-specific full path of the directory containing the filepath.

symbols.description:
Gets the filesystem paths that will be searched when require is called.

The paths string returned by this function is a sequence of path templates separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory separators.)

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

returns.name: paths
returns.description: The paths that the ''require'' function will check in love's filesystem.

symbols.name: getSaveDirectory
symbols.description:
Gets the full path to the designated save directory.

This can be useful if you want to use the standard io library (or something else) to

read or write in the save directory.

returns.name: dir
returns.description: The absolute path to the save directory.

symbols.name: getSource
symbols.description: Returns the full path to the the .love file or directory. If the game is fused to the LÖVE executable, then the executable is returned.

returns.name: path
returns.description: The full platform-dependent path of the .love file or directory.

symbols.name: getSourceBaseDirectory
symbols.description:
Returns the full path to the directory containing the .love file. If the game is fused to the LÖVE executable, then the directory containing the executable is returned.

If love.filesystem.isFused is true, the path returned by this function can be passed to love.filesystem.mount, which will make the directory containing the main game (e.g. C:\Program Files\coolgame\) readable by love.filesystem.

returns.name: path
returns.description: The full platform-dependent path of the directory containing the .love file.

arguments.name: appname
arguments.description: The name of the application binary, typically love.

symbols.name: isFused
symbols.description:
Gets whether the game is in fused mode or not.

If a game is in fused mode, its save directory will be directly in the Appdata directory instead of Appdata/LOVE/. The game will also be able to load C Lua dynamic libraries which are located in the save directory.

A game is in fused mode if the source .love has been fused to the executable (see Game Distribution), or if '--fused' has been given as a command-line argument when starting the game.

returns.name: fused
returns.description: True if the game is in fused mode, false otherwise.

symbols.name: mount

Mounts a zip file or folder in the game's save directory for reading.

It is also possible to mount love.filesystem.getSourceBaseDirectory if the game is in fused mode.

arguments.description: The new path the archive will be mounted to.

arguments.name: appendToPath
arguments.description: Whether the archive will be searched when reading a filepath before or after already-mounted archives. This includes the game's source and save directories.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the archive was successfully mounted, false otherwise.

arguments.name: mountpoint
arguments.description: The new path the archive will be mounted to.

arguments.description: The new path the archive will be mounted to.

arguments.description: Whether the archive will be searched when reading a filepath before or after already-mounted archives. This includes the game's source and save directories.

returns.description: True if the archive was successfully mounted, false otherwise.

Applies the given Transform object to the current coordinate transformation.

This effectively multiplies the existing coordinate transformation's matrix with the Transform object's internal matrix to produce the new coordinate transformation.

symbols.name: captureScreenshot
symbols.description:
Creates a screenshot once the current frame is done (after love.draw has finished).

Since this function enqueues a screenshot capture rather than executing it immediately, it can be called from an input callback or love.update and it will still capture all of what's drawn to the screen in that frame.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filename to save the screenshot to. The encoded image type is determined based on the extension of the filename, and must be one of the ImageFormats.

arguments.name: callback
arguments.description: Function which gets called once the screenshot has been captured. An ImageData is passed into the function as its only argument.

arguments.name: channel
arguments.description: The Channel to push the generated ImageData to.

symbols.name: clear
symbols.description:
Clears the screen or active Canvas to the specified color.

This function is called automatically before love.draw in the default love.run function. See the example in love.run for a typical use of this function.

Note that the scissor area bounds the cleared region.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

In versions prior to background color instead.

arguments.description: The red channel of the color to clear the screen to.

arguments.name: g
arguments.description: The green channel of the color to clear the screen to.

arguments.name: b
arguments.description: The blue channel of the color to clear the screen to.

arguments.name: a
arguments.description: The alpha channel of the color to clear the screen to.

arguments.name: clearstencil
arguments.description: Whether to clear the active stencil buffer, if present. It can also be an integer between 0 and 255 to clear the stencil buffer to a specific value.

arguments.name: cleardepth
arguments.description: Whether to clear the active depth buffer, if present. It can also be a number between 0 and 1 to clear the depth buffer to a specific value.

arguments.name: color
arguments.description: A table in the form of {r, g, b, a} containing the color to clear the first active Canvas to.
Additional tables for each active Canvas.

arguments.name: clearcolor
arguments.description: Whether to clear the active color canvas to transparent black (0, 0, 0, 0). Typically this should be set to false with this variant of the function.

symbols.name: discard
symbols.description:
Discards (trashes) the contents of the screen or active Canvas. This is a performance optimization function with niche use cases.

If the active Canvas has just been changed and the 'replace' BlendMode is about to be used to draw something which covers the entire screen, calling love.graphics.discard rather than calling love.graphics.clear or doing nothing may improve performance on mobile devices.

On some desktop systems this function may do nothing.

arguments.name: discardcolor
arguments.description: Whether to discard the texture(s) of the active Canvas(es) (the contents of the screen if no Canvas is active.)

arguments.name: discardstencil
arguments.description: Whether to discard the contents of the stencil buffer of the screen / active Canvas.

arguments.name: discardcolors
arguments.description: An array containing boolean values indicating whether to discard the texture of each active Canvas, when multiple simultaneous Canvases are active.

arguments.name: discardstencil
arguments.description: Whether to discard the contents of the stencil buffer of the screen / active Canvas.

symbols.name: draw
symbols.description:
Draws a Drawable object (an Image, Canvas, SpriteBatch, ParticleSystem, Mesh, Text object, or Video) on the screen with optional rotation, scaling and shearing.

Objects are drawn relative to their local coordinate system. The origin is by default located at the top left corner of Image and Canvas. All scaling, shearing, and rotation arguments transform the object relative to that point. Also, the position of the origin can be specified on the screen coordinate system.

It's possible to rotate an object about its center by offsetting the origin to the center. Angles must be given in radians for rotation. One can also use a negative scaling factor to flip about its centerline.

Note that the offsets are applied before rotation, scaling, or shearing; scaling and shearing are applied before rotation.

The right and bottom edges of the object are shifted at an angle defined by the shearing factors.

When using the default shader anything drawn with this function will be tinted according to the currently selected color.  Set it to pure white to preserve the object's original colors.

arguments.description: A drawable object.

symbols.name: drawInstanced
symbols.description:
Draws many instances of a Mesh with a single draw call, using hardware geometry instancing.

Each instance can have unique properties (positions, colors, etc.) but will not by default unless a custom per-instance vertex attributes or the love_InstanceID GLSL 3 vertex shader variable is used, otherwise they will all render at the same position on top of each other.

Instancing is not supported by some older GPUs that are only capable of using OpenGL ES 2 or OpenGL 2. Use love.graphics.getSupported to check.

symbols.name: flushBatch
symbols.description:
Immediately renders any pending automatically batched draws.

LÖVE will call this function internally as needed when most state is changed, so it is not necessary to manually call it.

The current batch will be automatically flushed by love.graphics state changes (except for the transform stack and the current color), as well as Shader:send and methods on Textures which change their state. Using a different Image in consecutive love.graphics.draw calls will also flush the current batch.

SpriteBatches, ParticleSystems, Meshes, and Text objects do their own batching and do not affect automatic batching of other draws, aside from flushing the current batch when they're drawn.

Gets the current background color.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

returns.name: formats

symbols.description:
Gets the current color.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

returns.name: r

returns.name: g
returns.description: The green component (0-1).

returns.name: b

symbols.name: getColorMask
symbols.description:
Gets the active color components used when drawing. Normally all 4 components are active unless love.graphics.setColorMask has been used.

The color mask determines whether individual components of the colors of drawn objects will affect the color of the screen. They affect love.graphics.clear and Canvas:clear as well.

returns.name: r
returns.description: Whether the red color component is active when rendering.

returns.name: g
returns.description: Whether the green color component is active when rendering.

returns.name: b
returns.description: Whether the blue color component is active when rendering.

returns.name: a
returns.description: Whether the alpha color component is active when rendering.

symbols.name: getDPIScale
symbols.description:
Gets the DPI scale factor of the window.

The DPI scale factor represents relative pixel density. The pixel density inside the window might be greater (or smaller) than the 'size' of the window. For example on a retina screen in Mac OS X with the highdpi window flag enabled, the window may take up the same physical size as an 800x600 window, but the area inside the window uses 1600x1200 pixels. love.graphics.getDPIScale() would return 2 in that case.

The love.window.fromPixels and love.window.toPixels functions can also be used to convert between units.

The highdpi window flag must be enabled to use the full pixel density of a Retina screen on Mac OS X and iOS. The flag currently does nothing on Windows and Linux, and on Android it is effectively always enabled.

returns.name: scale
returns.description: The pixel scale factor associated with the window.

symbols.name: getDefaultFilter
symbols.description: Returns the default scaling filters used with Images, Canvases, and Fonts.

returns.name: min
returns.description: Filter mode used when scaling the image down.

returns.name: mag
returns.description: Filter mode used when scaling the image up.

returns.name: anisotropy
returns.description: Maximum amount of Anisotropic Filtering used.

symbols.name: getDepthMode
symbols.description:
Gets the current depth test mode and whether writing to the depth buffer is enabled.

This is low-level functionality designed for use with custom vertex shaders and Meshes with custom vertex attributes. No higher level APIs are provided to set the depth of 2D graphics such as shapes, lines, and Images.

returns.name: comparemode
returns.description: Depth comparison mode used for depth testing.

returns.name: write
returns.description: Whether to write update / write values to the depth buffer when rendering.

symbols.name: getDimensions
symbols.description: Gets the width and height in pixels of the window.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the window.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of the window.

symbols.name: getFont
symbols.description: Gets the current Font object.

returns.name: font
returns.description: The current Font. Automatically creates and sets the default font, if none is set yet.

symbols.name: getFrontFaceWinding
symbols.description:
Gets whether triangles with clockwise- or counterclockwise-ordered vertices are considered front-facing.

This is designed for use in combination with Mesh face culling. Other love.graphics shapes, lines, and sprites are not guaranteed to have a specific winding order to their internal vertices.

returns.name: winding
returns.description: The winding mode being used. The default winding is counterclockwise ('ccw').

symbols.name: getHeight
symbols.description: Gets the height in pixels of the window.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of the window.

symbols.name: getImageFormats
symbols.description: Gets the raw and compressed pixel formats usable for Images, and whether each is supported.

returns.name: formats
returns.description: A table containing PixelFormats as keys, and a boolean indicating whether the format is supported as values. Not all systems support all formats.

symbols.name: getLineJoin
symbols.description: Gets the line join style.

returns.name: join
returns.description: The LineJoin style.

symbols.name: getLineStyle
symbols.description: Gets the line style.

returns.name: style
returns.description: The current line style.

symbols.name: getLineWidth
symbols.description: Gets the current line width.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The current line width.

symbols.name: getMeshCullMode
symbols.description:
Gets whether back-facing triangles in a Mesh are culled.

Mesh face culling is designed for use with low level custom hardware-accelerated 3D rendering via custom vertex attributes on Meshes, custom vertex shaders, and depth testing with a depth buffer.

returns.name: mode
returns.description: The Mesh face culling mode in use (whether to render everything, cull back-facing triangles, or cull front-facing triangles).

symbols.name: getPixelDimensions
symbols.description:
Gets the width and height in pixels of the window.

love.graphics.getDimensions gets the dimensions of the window in units scaled by the screen's DPI scale factor, rather than pixels. Use getDimensions for calculations related to drawing to the screen and using the graphics coordinate system (calculating the center of the screen, for example), and getPixelDimensions only when dealing specifically with underlying pixels (pixel-related calculations in a pixel Shader, for example).

returns.name: pixelwidth
returns.description: The width of the window in pixels.

returns.name: pixelheight
returns.description: The height of the window in pixels.

symbols.name: getPixelHeight
symbols.description:
Gets the height in pixels of the window.

The graphics coordinate system and DPI scale factor, rather than raw pixels. Use getHeight for calculations related to drawing to the screen and using the coordinate system (calculating the center of the screen, for example), and getPixelHeight only when dealing specifically with underlying pixels (pixel-related calculations in a pixel Shader, for example).

returns.name: pixelheight
returns.description: The height of the window in pixels.

symbols.name: getPixelWidth
symbols.description:
Gets the width in pixels of the window.

The graphics coordinate system and DPI scale factor, rather than raw pixels. Use getWidth for calculations related to drawing to the screen and using the coordinate system (calculating the center of the screen, for example), and getPixelWidth only when dealing specifically with underlying pixels (pixel-related calculations in a pixel Shader, for example).

returns.name: pixelwidth
returns.description: The width of the window in pixels.

returns.description: The current point size.

returns.name: vendor

returns.name: device
returns.description: The name of the graphics card, e.g. 'Intel HD Graphics 3000 OpenGL Engine'.

returns.description: The table that was passed in above, now containing the following fields:

symbols.name: getStencilTest
symbols.description:
Gets the current stencil test configuration.

When stencil testing is enabled, the geometry of everything that is drawn afterward will be clipped / stencilled out based on a comparison between the arguments of this function and the stencil value of each pixel that the geometry touches. The stencil values of pixels are affected via love.graphics.stencil.

Each Canvas has its own per-pixel stencil values.

returns.name: comparemode
returns.description: The type of comparison that is made for each pixel. Will be 'always' if stencil testing is disabled.

returns.name: comparevalue
returns.description: The value used when comparing with the stencil value of each pixel.

symbols.name: getSupported
symbols.description:
Gets the optional graphics features and whether they're supported on the system.

Some older or low-end systems don't always support all graphics features.

returns.name: features
returns.description: A table containing GraphicsFeature keys, and boolean values indicating whether each feature is supported.

symbols.name: getSystemLimits
symbols.description: Gets the system-dependent maximum values for love.graphics features.

returns.name: limits
returns.description: A table containing GraphicsLimit keys, and number values.

symbols.name: getTextureTypes
symbols.description: Gets the available texture types, and whether each is supported.

returns.name: texturetypes
returns.description: A table containing TextureTypes as keys, and a boolean indicating whether the type is supported as values. Not all systems support all types.

symbols.name: getWidth
symbols.description: Gets the width in pixels of the window.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the window.

symbols.name: intersectScissor
symbols.description:
Sets the scissor to the rectangle created by the intersection of the specified rectangle with the existing scissor.  If no scissor is active yet, it behaves like love.graphics.setScissor.

The scissor limits the drawing area to a specified rectangle. This affects all graphics calls, including love.graphics.clear.

The dimensions of the scissor is unaffected by graphical transformations (translate, scale, ...).

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-coordinate of the upper left corner of the rectangle to intersect with the existing scissor rectangle.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-coordinate of the upper left corner of the rectangle to intersect with the existing scissor rectangle.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The width of the rectangle to intersect with the existing scissor rectangle.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The height of the rectangle to intersect with the existing scissor rectangle.

symbols.name: inverseTransformPoint
symbols.description:
Converts the given 2D position from screen-space into global coordinates.

This effectively applies the reverse of the current graphics transformations to the given position. A similar Transform:inverseTransformPoint method exists for Transform objects.

arguments.name: screenX
arguments.description: The x component of the screen-space position.

arguments.name: screenY
arguments.description: The y component of the screen-space position.

returns.name: globalX
returns.description: The x component of the position in global coordinates.

returns.name: globalY
returns.description: The y component of the position in global coordinates.

symbols.name: isActive
symbols.description:
Gets whether the graphics module is able to be used. If it is not active, love.graphics function and method calls will not work correctly and may cause the program to crash.
The graphics module is inactive if a window is not open, or if the app is in the background on iOS. Typically the app's execution will be automatically paused by the system, in the latter case.

returns.name: active
returns.description: Whether the graphics module is active and able to be used.

symbols.name: isGammaCorrect
symbols.description:
Gets whether gamma-correct rendering is supported and enabled. It can be enabled by setting t.gammacorrect = true in love.conf.

Not all devices support gamma-correct rendering, in which case it will be automatically disabled and this function will return false. It is supported on desktop systems which have graphics cards that are capable of using OpenGL 3 / DirectX 10, and iOS devices that can use OpenGL ES 3.

returns.name: gammacorrect
returns.description: True if gamma-correct rendering is supported and was enabled in love.conf, false otherwise.

symbols.name: isWireframe
symbols.description: Gets whether wireframe mode is used when drawing.

returns.name: wireframe
returns.description: True if wireframe lines are used when drawing, false if it's not.

symbols.name: line
symbols.description: Draws lines between points.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The position of first point on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The position of first point on the y-axis.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The position of second point on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The position of second point on the y-axis.
You can continue passing point positions to draw a polyline.

arguments.name: points
arguments.description: A table of point positions, as described above.

symbols.name: newArrayImage
symbols.description:
Creates a new array Image.

An array image / array texture is a single object which contains multiple 'layers' or 'slices' of 2D sub-images. It can be thought of similarly to a texture atlas or sprite sheet, but it doesn't suffer from the same tile / quad bleeding artifacts that texture atlases do – although every sub-image must have the same dimensions.

A specific layer of an array image can be drawn with love.graphics.drawLayer / SpriteBatch:addLayer, or with the Quad variant of love.graphics.draw and Quad:setLayer, or via a custom Shader.

To use an array image in a Shader, it must be declared as a ArrayImage or sampler2DArray type (instead of Image or sampler2D). The Texel(ArrayImage image, vec3 texturecoord) shader function must be used to get pixel colors from a slice of the array image. The vec3 argument contains the texture coordinate in the first two components, and the 0-based slice index in the third component.

arguments.name: slices
arguments.description: A table containing filepaths to images (or File, FileData, ImageData, or CompressedImageData objects), in an array. Each sub-image must have the same dimensions. A table of tables can also be given, where each sub-table contains all mipmap levels for the slice index of that sub-table.

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: Optional table of settings to configure the array image, containing the following fields:

returns.name: image
returns.description: An Array Image object.

arguments.name: height

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: A table containing the given fields:

returns.name: canvas
returns.description: A new Canvas with specified width and height.

symbols.name: newCubeImage
symbols.description:
Creates a new cubemap Image.

Cubemap images have 6 faces (sides) which represent a cube. They can't be rendered directly, they can only be used in Shader code (and sent to the shader via Shader:send).

To use a cubemap image in a Shader, it must be declared as a CubeImage or samplerCube type (instead of Image or sampler2D). The Texel(CubeImage image, vec3 direction) shader function must be used to get pixel colors from the cubemap. The vec3 argument is a normalized direction from the center of the cube, rather than explicit texture coordinates.

Each face in a cubemap image must have square dimensions.

For variants of this function which accept a single image containing multiple cubemap faces, they must be laid out in one of the following forms in the image:

+y

+z +x -z

-y

-x

or:

+y

-x +z +x -z

-y

or:

+x

-x

+y

-y

+z

-z

or:

+x -x +y -y +z -z

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filepath to a cubemap image file (or a File, FileData, or ImageData).

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: Optional table of settings to configure the cubemap image, containing the following fields:

returns.name: image
returns.description: An cubemap Image object.

arguments.name: faces
arguments.description: A table containing 6 filepaths to images (or File, FileData, ImageData, or CompressedImageData objects), in an array. Each face image must have the same dimensions. A table of tables can also be given, where each sub-table contains all mipmap levels for the cube face index of that sub-table.

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: Optional table of settings to configure the cubemap image, containing the following fields:

returns.name: image
returns.description: An cubemap Image object.

Creates a new Font from a TrueType Font or BMFont file. Created fonts are not cached, in that calling this function with the same arguments will always create a new Font object.

symbols.name: newMesh
symbols.description:
Creates a new Mesh.

Use Mesh:setTexture if the Mesh should be textured with an Image or Canvas when it's drawn.

In versions prior to 11.0, color and byte component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

arguments.name: usage

returns.name: mesh
returns.description: The new mesh.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How the vertices are used when drawing. The default mode 'fan' is sufficient for simple convex polygons.

arguments.description: The expected usage of the Mesh. The specified usage mode affects the Mesh's memory usage and performance.

arguments.description: A table in the form of {attribute, ...}. Each attribute is a table which specifies a custom vertex attribute used for each vertex.

arguments.name: mode

returns.name: mesh
returns.description: The new mesh.

arguments.description: How the vertices are used when drawing. The default mode 'fan' is sufficient for simple convex polygons.

arguments.name: usage

symbols.name: newQuad
symbols.description:
Creates a new Quad.

The purpose of a Quad is to use a fraction of an image to draw objects, as opposed to drawing entire image. It is most useful for sprite sheets and atlases: in a sprite atlas, multiple sprites reside in same image, quad is used to draw a specific sprite from that image; in animated sprites with all frames residing in the same image, quad is used to draw specific frame from the animation.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The top-left position in the Image along the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The top-left position in the Image along the y-axis.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The width of the Quad in the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The height of the Quad in the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

arguments.name: sw
arguments.description: The reference width, the width of the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

arguments.name: sh
arguments.description: The reference height, the height of the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

returns.name: quad
returns.description: The new Quad.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The top-left position in the Image along the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The top-left position in the Image along the y-axis.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The width of the Quad in the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The height of the Quad in the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

arguments.name: texture
arguments.description: The texture whose width and height will be used as the reference width and height.

returns.name: quad
returns.description: The new Quad.

symbols.name: newShader
symbols.description:
Creates a new Shader object for hardware-accelerated vertex and pixel effects. A Shader contains either vertex shader code, pixel shader code, or both.

Shaders are small programs which are run on the graphics card when drawing. Vertex shaders are run once for each vertex (for example, an image has 4 vertices - one at each corner. A Mesh might have many more.) Pixel shaders are run once for each pixel on the screen which the drawn object touches. Pixel shader code is executed after all the object's vertices have been processed by the vertex shader.

arguments.name: code
arguments.description: The pixel shader or vertex shader code, or a filename pointing to a file with the code.

returns.name: shader
returns.description: A Shader object for use in drawing operations.

arguments.name: pixelcode
arguments.description: The pixel shader code, or a filename pointing to a file with the code.

arguments.name: vertexcode
arguments.description: The vertex shader code, or a filename pointing to a file with the code.

returns.name: shader
returns.description: A Shader object for use in drawing operations.

symbols.description: Creates a new SpriteBatch object.

arguments.description: The maximum number of sprites that the SpriteBatch can contain at any given time. Since version 11.0, additional sprites added past this number will automatically grow the spritebatch.

returns.name: spriteBatch
returns.description: The new SpriteBatch.

arguments.name: image
arguments.description: The Image to use for the sprites.

arguments.name: maxsprites
arguments.description: The maximum number of sprites that the SpriteBatch can contain at any given time. Since version 11.0, additional sprites added past this number will automatically grow the spritebatch.

arguments.name: usage
arguments.description: The expected usage of the SpriteBatch. The specified usage mode affects the SpriteBatch's memory usage and performance.

returns.name: spriteBatch

returns.description: The new SpriteBatch.

symbols.name: newVolumeImage
symbols.description:
Creates a new volume (3D) Image.

Volume images are 3D textures with width, height, and depth. They can't be rendered directly, they can only be used in Shader code (and sent to the shader via Shader:send).

To use a volume image in a Shader, it must be declared as a VolumeImage or sampler3D type (instead of Image or sampler2D). The Texel(VolumeImage image, vec3 texcoords) shader function must be used to get pixel colors from the volume image. The vec3 argument is a normalized texture coordinate with the z component representing the depth to sample at (ranging from 1).

Volume images are typically used as lookup tables in shaders for color grading, for example, because sampling using a texture coordinate that is partway in between two pixels can interpolate across all 3 dimensions in the volume image, resulting in a smooth gradient even when a small-sized volume image is used as the lookup table.

Array images are a much better choice than volume images for storing multiple different sprites in a single array image for directly drawing them.

arguments.name: layers
arguments.description: A table containing filepaths to images (or File, FileData, ImageData, or CompressedImageData objects), in an array. A table of tables can also be given, where each sub-table represents a single mipmap level and contains all layers for that mipmap.

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: Optional table of settings to configure the volume image, containing the following fields:

returns.name: image
returns.description: A volume Image object.

symbols.name: origin
symbols.description:
Resets the current coordinate transformation.

This function is always used to reverse any previous calls to love.graphics.rotate, love.graphics.scale, love.graphics.shear or love.graphics.translate. It returns the current transformation state to its defaults.

symbols.name: points
symbols.description: Draws one or more points.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The position of the first point on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The position of the first point on the y-axis.
The x and y coordinates of additional points.

arguments.name: points
arguments.description: A table containing multiple point positions, in the form of {x, y, ...}.

arguments.name: points
arguments.description: A table containing multiple individually colored points, in the form of {point, ...}.

symbols.name: polygon
symbols.description:
Draw a polygon.

Following the mode argument, this function can accept multiple numeric arguments or a single table of numeric arguments. In either case the arguments are interpreted as alternating x and y coordinates of the polygon's vertices.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How to draw the polygon.
The vertices of the polygon.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How to draw the polygon.

arguments.name: vertices
arguments.description: The vertices of the polygon as a table.

symbols.name: pop
symbols.description:
Pops the current coordinate transformation from the transformation stack.

This function is always used to reverse a previous push operation. It returns the current transformation state to what it was before the last preceding push.

symbols.name: present
symbols.description:
Displays the results of drawing operations on the screen.

This function is used when writing your own love.run function. It presents all the results of your drawing operations on the screen. See the example in love.run for a typical use of this function.

symbols.name: print
symbols.description:
Draws text on screen. If no Font is set, one will be created and set (once) if needed.

As of LOVE 0.7.1, when using translation and scaling functions while drawing text, this function assumes the scale occurs first.  If you don't script with this in mind, the text won't be in the right position, or possibly even on screen.

love.graphics.print and love.graphics.printf both support UTF-8 encoding. You'll also need a proper Font for special characters.

In versions prior to 11.0, color and byte component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

arguments.name: text
arguments.description: The text to draw.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The position to draw the object (x-axis).

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The position to draw the object (y-axis).

arguments.name: r
arguments.description: Orientation (radians).

arguments.name: sx
arguments.description: Scale factor (x-axis).

arguments.name: sy
arguments.description: Scale factor (y-axis).

arguments.name: ox
arguments.description: Origin offset (x-axis).

arguments.name: oy
arguments.description: Origin offset (y-axis).

arguments.name: kx
arguments.description: Shearing factor (x-axis).

arguments.name: ky
arguments.description: Shearing factor (y-axis).

arguments.name: coloredtext
arguments.description: A table containing colors and strings to add to the object, in the form of {color1, string1, color2, string2, ...}.

arguments.name: x

symbols.name: printf
symbols.description:
Draws formatted text, with word wrap and alignment.

See additional notes in love.graphics.print.

The word wrap limit is applied before any scaling, rotation, and other coordinate transformations. Therefore the amount of text per line stays constant given the same wrap limit, even if the scale arguments change.

In version 0.9.2 and earlier, wrapping was implemented by breaking up words by spaces and putting them back together to make sure things fit nicely within the limit provided. However, due to the way this is done, extra spaces between words would end up missing when printed on the screen, and some lines could overflow past the provided wrap limit. In version 0.10.0 and newer this is no longer the case.

In versions prior to 11.0, color and byte component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.
