symbols.description: Gets the current vibration motor strengths on a Joystick with rumble support.

returns.name: left
returns.description: Current strength of the left vibration motor on the Joystick.

returns.name: right
returns.description: Current strength of the right vibration motor on the Joystick.

symbols.name: isConnected
symbols.description: Gets whether the Joystick is connected.

returns.name: connected
returns.description: True if the Joystick is currently connected, false otherwise.

symbols.name: isDown
symbols.description:
Checks if a button on the Joystick is pressed.

LÖVE 0.9.0 had a bug which required the button indices passed to Joystick:isDown to be 0-based instead of 1-based, for example button 1 would be 0 for this function. It was fixed in 0.9.1.

arguments.name: buttonN
arguments.description: The index of a button to check.

returns.name: anyDown
returns.description: True if any supplied button is down, false if not.

symbols.name: isGamepad
symbols.description:
Gets whether the Joystick is recognized as a gamepad. If this is the case, the Joystick's buttons and axes can be used in a standardized manner across different operating systems and joystick models via Joystick:getGamepadAxis, Joystick:isGamepadDown, love.gamepadpressed, and related functions.

LÖVE automatically recognizes most popular controllers with a similar layout to the Xbox 360 controller as gamepads, but you can add more with love.joystick.setGamepadMapping.

returns.name: isgamepad
returns.description: True if the Joystick is recognized as a gamepad, false otherwise.

symbols.name: isGamepadDown
symbols.description: Checks if a virtual gamepad button on the Joystick is pressed. If the Joystick is not recognized as a Gamepad or isn't connected, then this function will always return false.

arguments.name: buttonN
arguments.description: The gamepad button to check.

returns.name: anyDown
returns.description: True if any supplied button is down, false if not.

symbols.name: isVibrationSupported
symbols.description: Gets whether the Joystick supports vibration.

returns.name: supported
returns.description: True if rumble / force feedback vibration is supported on this Joystick, false if not.

symbols.name: setVibration
symbols.description: Sets the vibration motor speeds on a Joystick with rumble support. Most common gamepads have this functionality, although not all drivers give proper support. Use Joystick:isVibrationSupported to check.

arguments.name: left
arguments.description: Strength of the left vibration motor on the Joystick. Must be in the range of 1.

arguments.name: right
arguments.description: Strength of the right vibration motor on the Joystick. Must be in the range of 1.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the vibration was successfully applied, false if not.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the vibration was successfully disabled, false if not.

arguments.name: left
arguments.description: Strength of the left vibration motor on the Joystick. Must be in the range of 1.

arguments.name: right
arguments.description: Strength of the right vibration motor on the Joystick. Must be in the range of 1.

arguments.name: duration
arguments.description: The duration of the vibration in seconds. A negative value means infinite duration.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the vibration was successfully applied, false if not.

symbols.name: getKeyFromScancode
symbols.description:
Gets the key corresponding to the given hardware scancode.

Unlike key constants, Scancodes are keyboard layout-independent. For example the scancode 'w' will be generated if the key in the same place as the 'w' key on an American keyboard is pressed, no matter what the key is labelled or what the user's operating system settings are.

Scancodes are useful for creating default controls that have the same physical locations on on all systems.

arguments.name: scancode
arguments.description: The scancode to get the key from.

returns.name: key
returns.description: The key corresponding to the given scancode, or 'unknown' if the scancode doesn't map to a KeyConstant on the current system.

symbols.name: getScancodeFromKey
symbols.description:
Gets the hardware scancode corresponding to the given key.

Unlike key constants, Scancodes are keyboard layout-independent. For example the scancode 'w' will be generated if the key in the same place as the 'w' key on an American keyboard is pressed, no matter what the key is labelled or what the user's operating system settings are.

Scancodes are useful for creating default controls that have the same physical locations on on all systems.

arguments.name: key
arguments.description: The key to get the scancode from.

returns.name: scancode
returns.description: The scancode corresponding to the given key, or 'unknown' if the given key has no known physical representation on the current system.

symbols.name: hasKeyRepeat
symbols.description: Gets whether key repeat is enabled.

returns.name: enabled
returns.description: Whether key repeat is enabled.

symbols.name: hasScreenKeyboard
symbols.description: Gets whether screen keyboard is supported.

returns.name: supported
returns.description: Whether screen keyboard is supported.

symbols.name: hasTextInput
symbols.description: Gets whether text input events are enabled.

returns.name: enabled
returns.description: Whether text input events are enabled.

symbols.name: isDown
symbols.description: Checks whether a certain key is down. Not to be confused with love.keypressed or love.keyreleased.

arguments.name: key
arguments.description: The key to check.

returns.name: down
returns.description: True if the key is down, false if not.

arguments.name: key
arguments.description: A key to check.
Additional keys to check.

returns.name: anyDown
returns.description: True if any supplied key is down, false if not.

symbols.name: isScancodeDown
symbols.description:
Checks whether the specified Scancodes are pressed. Not to be confused with love.keypressed or love.keyreleased.

Unlike regular KeyConstants, Scancodes are keyboard layout-independent. The scancode 'w' is used if the key in the same place as the 'w' key on an American keyboard is pressed, no matter what the key is labelled or what the user's operating system settings are.

arguments.name: scancode
arguments.description: A Scancode to check.
Additional Scancodes to check.

returns.name: down
returns.description: True if any supplied Scancode is down, false if not.

symbols.name: setKeyRepeat
symbols.description: Enables or disables key repeat for love.keypressed. It is disabled by default.

arguments.name: enable
arguments.description: Whether repeat keypress events should be enabled when a key is held down.

symbols.name: setTextInput
symbols.description:
Enables or disables text input events. It is enabled by default on Windows, Mac, and Linux, and disabled by default on iOS and Android.

On touch devices, this shows the system's native on-screen keyboard when it's enabled.

arguments.name: enable
arguments.description: Whether text input events should be enabled.

arguments.name: enable
arguments.description: Whether text input events should be enabled.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: Text rectangle x position.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: Text rectangle y position.

arguments.name: w
arguments.description: Text rectangle width.

arguments.name: h
arguments.description: Text rectangle height.

symbols.name: colorFromBytes
symbols.description: Converts a color from 0..255 to 0..1 range.

arguments.name: rb
arguments.description: Red color component in 0..255 range.

arguments.name: gb
arguments.description: Green color component in 0..255 range.

arguments.name: bb
arguments.description: Blue color component in 0..255 range.

arguments.name: ab
arguments.description: Alpha color component in 0..255 range.

returns.name: r
returns.description: Red color component in 0..1 range.

returns.name: g
returns.description: Green color component in 0..1 range.

returns.name: b
returns.description: Blue color component in 0..1 range.

returns.name: a
returns.description: Alpha color component in 0..1 range or nil if alpha is not specified.

symbols.name: colorToBytes
symbols.description: Converts a color from 0..1 to 0..255 range.

arguments.name: r
arguments.description: Red color component.

arguments.name: g
arguments.description: Green color component.

arguments.name: b
arguments.description: Blue color component.

arguments.name: a
arguments.description: Alpha color component.

returns.name: rb
returns.description: Red color component in 0..255 range.

returns.name: gb
returns.description: Green color component in 0..255 range.

returns.name: bb
returns.description: Blue color component in 0..255 range.

returns.name: ab
returns.description: Alpha color component in 0..255 range or nil if alpha is not specified.

symbols.name: gammaToLinear
symbols.description:
Converts a color from gamma-space (sRGB) to linear-space (RGB). This is useful when doing gamma-correct rendering and you need to do math in linear RGB in the few cases where LÖVE doesn't handle conversions automatically.

Read more about gamma-correct rendering here, here, and here.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

arguments.name: r
arguments.description: The red channel of the sRGB color to convert.

arguments.name: g
arguments.description: The green channel of the sRGB color to convert.

arguments.name: b
arguments.description: The blue channel of the sRGB color to convert.

returns.name: lr
returns.description: The red channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

returns.name: lg
returns.description: The green channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

returns.name: lb
returns.description: The blue channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

arguments.name: color
arguments.description: An array with the red, green, and blue channels of the sRGB color to convert.

returns.name: lr
returns.description: The red channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

returns.name: lg
returns.description: The green channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

returns.name: lb
returns.description: The blue channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

arguments.name: c
arguments.description: The value of a color channel in sRGB space to convert.

returns.name: lc
returns.description: The value of the color channel in linear RGB space.

symbols.name: getRandomSeed
symbols.description:
Gets the seed of the random number generator.

The seed is split into two numbers due to Lua's use of doubles for all number values - doubles can't accurately represent integer  values above 2^53, but the seed can be an integer value up to 2^64.

returns.name: low
returns.description: Integer number representing the lower 32 bits of the random number generator's 64 bit seed value.

returns.name: high
returns.description: Integer number representing the higher 32 bits of the random number generator's 64 bit seed value.

symbols.name: getRandomState
symbols.description:
Gets the current state of the random number generator. This returns an opaque implementation-dependent string which is only useful for later use with love.math.setRandomState or RandomGenerator:setState.

This is different from love.math.getRandomSeed in that getRandomState gets the random number generator's current state, whereas getRandomSeed gets the previously set seed number.

returns.name: state
returns.description: The current state of the random number generator, represented as a string.

symbols.name: isConvex
symbols.description:
Checks whether a polygon is convex.

PolygonShapes in love.physics, some forms of Meshes, and polygons drawn with love.graphics.polygon must be simple convex polygons.

arguments.name: vertices
arguments.description: The vertices of the polygon as a table in the form of {x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3, ...}.

returns.name: convex
returns.description: Whether the given polygon is convex.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The position of the first vertex of the polygon on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The position of the first vertex of the polygon on the y-axis.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The position of the second vertex of the polygon on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The position of the second vertex of the polygon on the y-axis.
Additional position of the vertex of the polygon on the x-axis and y-axis.

returns.name: convex
returns.description: Whether the given polygon is convex.

symbols.name: linearToGamma
symbols.description:
Converts a color from linear-space (RGB) to gamma-space (sRGB). This is useful when storing linear RGB color values in an image, because the linear RGB color space has less precision than sRGB for dark colors, which can result in noticeable color banding when drawing.

In general, colors chosen based on what they look like on-screen are already in gamma-space and should not be double-converted. Colors calculated using math are often in the linear RGB space.

Read more about gamma-correct rendering here, here, and here.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

arguments.name: lr
arguments.description: The red channel of the linear RGB color to convert.

arguments.name: lg
arguments.description: The green channel of the linear RGB color to convert.

arguments.name: lb
arguments.description: The blue channel of the linear RGB color to convert.

returns.name: cr
returns.description: The red channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

returns.name: cg
returns.description: The green channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

returns.name: cb
returns.description: The blue channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

arguments.name: color
arguments.description: An array with the red, green, and blue channels of the linear RGB color to convert.

returns.name: cr
returns.description: The red channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

returns.name: cg
returns.description: The green channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

returns.name: cb
returns.description: The blue channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

arguments.name: lc
arguments.description: The value of a color channel in linear RGB space to convert.

returns.name: c
returns.description: The value of the color channel in gamma sRGB space.

symbols.name: newBezierCurve
symbols.description:
Creates a new BezierCurve object.

The number of vertices in the control polygon determines the degree of the curve, e.g. three vertices define a quadratic (degree 2) Bézier curve, four vertices define a cubic (degree 3) Bézier curve, etc.

arguments.name: vertices
arguments.description: The vertices of the control polygon as a table in the form of {x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3, ...}.

returns.name: curve
returns.description: A Bézier curve object.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The position of the first vertex of the control polygon on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The position of the first vertex of the control polygon on the y-axis.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The position of the second vertex of the control polygon on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The position of the second vertex of the control polygon on the y-axis.
Additional position of the vertex of the control polygon on the x-axis and y-axis.

returns.name: curve
returns.description: A Bézier curve object.

symbols.name: newRandomGenerator
symbols.description: Creates a new RandomGenerator object which is completely independent of other RandomGenerator objects and random functions.

returns.name: rng
returns.description: The new Random Number Generator object.

arguments.name: low
arguments.description: The lower 32 bits of the seed number to use for this object.

arguments.name: high
arguments.description: The higher 32 bits of the seed number to use for this object.

returns.name: rng
returns.description: The new Random Number Generator object.

symbols.name: newTransform

arguments.name: x

arguments.name: y

arguments.name: angle

arguments.name: sx

arguments.name: sy

arguments.name: ox

arguments.name: oy

arguments.name: kx

arguments.name: ky

returns.name: transform
returns.description: The new Transform object.

symbols.name: noise
symbols.description:
Generates a Simplex or Perlin noise value in 1-4 dimensions. The return value will always be the same, given the same arguments.

Simplex noise is closely related to Perlin noise. It is widely used for procedural content generation.

There are many webpages which discuss Perlin and Simplex noise in detail.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The number used to generate the noise value.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The noise value in the range of 1.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The first value of the 2-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The second value of the 2-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The noise value in the range of 1.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The first value of the 3-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The second value of the 3-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

arguments.name: z
arguments.description: The third value of the 3-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The noise value in the range of 1.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The first value of the 4-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The second value of the 4-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

arguments.name: z
arguments.description: The third value of the 4-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

arguments.name: w
arguments.description: The fourth value of the 4-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The noise value in the range of 1.

symbols.name: random
symbols.description: Generates a pseudo-random number in a platform independent manner. The default love.run seeds this function at startup, so you generally don't need to seed it yourself.

returns.name: number
returns.description: The pseudo-random number.

arguments.name: max
arguments.description: The maximum possible value it should return.

returns.name: number
returns.description: The pseudo-random integer number.

arguments.name: min
arguments.description: The minimum possible value it should return.

arguments.name: max
arguments.description: The maximum possible value it should return.

returns.name: number
returns.description: The pseudo-random integer number.

symbols.name: randomNormal
symbols.description: Get a normally distributed pseudo random number.

arguments.name: stddev
arguments.description: Standard deviation of the distribution.

arguments.name: mean
arguments.description: The mean of the distribution.

returns.name: number
returns.description: Normally distributed random number with variance (stddev)² and the specified mean.

symbols.name: setRandomSeed
symbols.description: Sets the seed of the random number generator using the specified integer number. This is called internally at startup, so you generally don't need to call it yourself.

arguments.name: seed
arguments.description: The integer number with which you want to seed the randomization. Must be within the range of 2^53 - 1.

arguments.name: low
arguments.description: The lower 32 bits of the seed value. Must be within the range of 2^32 - 1.

arguments.name: high
arguments.description: The higher 32 bits of the seed value. Must be within the range of 2^32 - 1.

symbols.name: setRandomState
symbols.description:
Sets the current state of the random number generator. The value used as an argument for this function is an opaque implementation-dependent string and should only originate from a previous call to love.math.getRandomState.

This is different from love.math.setRandomSeed in that setRandomState directly sets the random number generator's current implementation-dependent state, whereas setRandomSeed gives it a new seed value.

arguments.name: state
arguments.description: The new state of the random number generator, represented as a string. This should originate from a previous call to love.math.getRandomState.

symbols.name: triangulate
symbols.description: Decomposes a simple convex or concave polygon into triangles.

returns.name: triangles

arguments.name: y1

arguments.name: y2

arguments.name: y3

returns.description: List of triangles the polygon is composed of, in the form of {{x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3},  {x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3}, ...}.

symbols.name: evaluate
symbols.description:
Evaluate Bézier curve at parameter t. The parameter must be between 0 and 1 (inclusive).

This function can be used to move objects along paths or tween parameters. However it should not be used to render the curve, see BezierCurve:render for that purpose.

arguments.name: t
arguments.description: Where to evaluate the curve.

returns.name: x
returns.description: x coordinate of the curve at parameter t.

returns.name: y
returns.description: y coordinate of the curve at parameter t.

symbols.name: getControlPoint
symbols.description: Get coordinates of the i-th control point. Indices start with 1.

arguments.name: i

returns.name: x
returns.description: Position of the control point along the x axis.

returns.name: y
returns.description: Position of the control point along the y axis.

symbols.description: Get the number of control points in the Bézier curve.

returns.name: count
returns.description: The number of control points.

symbols.name: getDegree
symbols.description: Get degree of the Bézier curve. The degree is equal to number-of-control-points - 1.

returns.name: degree
returns.description: Degree of the Bézier curve.

symbols.name: getDerivative
symbols.description:
Get the derivative of the Bézier curve.

This function can be used to rotate sprites moving along a curve in the direction of the movement and compute the direction perpendicular to the curve at some parameter t.

returns.name: derivative
returns.description: The derivative curve.

symbols.name: getSegment
symbols.description: Gets a BezierCurve that corresponds to the specified segment of this BezierCurve.

arguments.name: endpoint
arguments.description: The end of the segment. Must be between 0 and 1.

returns.name: curve
returns.description: A BezierCurve that corresponds to the specified segment.

symbols.name: insertControlPoint
symbols.description: Insert control point as the new i-th control point. Existing control points from i onwards are pushed back by 1. Indices start with 1. Negative indices wrap around: -1 is the last control point, -2 the one before the last, etc.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: Position of the control point along the x axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: Position of the control point along the y axis.

arguments.name: i
arguments.description: Index of the control point.

symbols.name: removeControlPoint
symbols.description: Removes the specified control point.

arguments.name: index
arguments.description: The index of the control point to remove.

symbols.name: render
symbols.description:
Get a list of coordinates to be used with love.graphics.line.

This function samples the Bézier curve using recursive subdivision. You can control the recursion depth using the depth parameter.

If you are just interested to know the position on the curve given a parameter, use BezierCurve:evaluate.

arguments.name: depth
arguments.description: Number of recursive subdivision steps.

returns.name: coordinates
returns.description: List of x,y-coordinate pairs of points on the curve.

symbols.name: renderSegment
symbols.description:
Get a list of coordinates on a specific part of the curve, to be used with love.graphics.line.

This function samples the Bézier curve using recursive subdivision. You can control the recursion depth using the depth parameter.

If you are just need to know the position on the curve given a parameter, use BezierCurve:evaluate.

arguments.name: startpoint
arguments.description: The starting point along the curve. Must be between 0 and 1.

arguments.name: endpoint
arguments.description: The end of the segment to render. Must be between 0 and 1.

arguments.name: depth
arguments.description: Number of recursive subdivision steps.

returns.name: coordinates
returns.description: List of x,y-coordinate pairs of points on the specified part of the curve.

Gets the seed of the random number generator object.

The seed is split into two numbers due to Lua's use of doubles for all number values - doubles can't accurately represent integer  values above 2^53, but the seed value is an integer number in the range of 2^64 - 1.

This is different from RandomGenerator:getSeed in that getState gets the RandomGenerator's current state, whereas getSeed gets the previously set seed number.

returns.name: state

symbols.name: setState

Sets the current state of the random number generator. The value used as an argument for this function is an opaque string and should only originate from a previous call to RandomGenerator:getState in the same major version of LÖVE.

symbols.name: apply
symbols.description:
Applies the given other Transform object to this one.

This effectively multiplies this Transform's internal transformation matrix with the other Transform's (i.e. self * other), and stores the result in this object.

arguments.name: other
arguments.description: The other Transform object to apply to this Transform.

returns.name: transform
returns.description: The Transform object the method was called on. Allows easily chaining Transform methods.

symbols.name: clone
symbols.description: Creates a new copy of this Transform.

returns.name: clone
returns.description: The copy of this Transform.

symbols.name: getMatrix
symbols.description: Gets the internal 4x4 transformation matrix stored by this Transform. The matrix is returned in row-major order.

returns.name: e1_1
returns.description: The first column of the first row of the matrix.

returns.name: e1_2
returns.description: The second column of the first row of the matrix.

returns.name: e1_3
returns.description: The third column of the first row of the matrix.

returns.name: e1_4
returns.description: The fourth column of the first row of the matrix.

returns.description: The first column of the second row of the matrix.

returns.name: e2_2
returns.description: The second column of the second row of the matrix.

returns.name: e2_3
returns.description: The third column of the second row of the matrix.

returns.name: e2_4
returns.description: The fourth column of the second row of the matrix.

returns.name: e3_1
returns.description: The first column of the third row of the matrix.

returns.name: e3_2
returns.description: The second column of the third row of the matrix.

returns.name: e3_3
returns.description: The third column of the third row of the matrix.

returns.name: e3_4
returns.description: The fourth column of the third row of the matrix.

returns.name: e4_1

returns.name: e4_2
returns.description: The second column of the fourth row of the matrix.

returns.name: e4_3
returns.description: The third column of the fourth row of the matrix.

returns.name: e4_4
returns.description: The fourth column of the fourth row of the matrix.

symbols.name: inverse
symbols.description: Creates a new Transform containing the inverse of this Transform.

returns.name: inverse
returns.description: A new Transform object representing the inverse of this Transform's matrix.

symbols.name: inverseTransformPoint
symbols.description:
Applies the reverse of the Transform object's transformation to the given 2D position.

This effectively converts the given position from the local coordinate space of the Transform into global coordinates.

One use of this method can be to convert a screen-space mouse position into global world coordinates, if the given Transform has transformations applied that are used for a camera system in-game.

arguments.name: localX
arguments.description: The x component of the position with the transform applied.

arguments.name: localY
arguments.description: The y component of the position with the transform applied.

returns.name: globalX
returns.description: The x component of the position in global coordinates.

returns.name: globalY
returns.description: The y component of the position in global coordinates.

symbols.name: isAffine2DTransform
symbols.description: Checks whether the Transform is an affine transformation.

returns.name: affine
returns.description: true if the transform object is an affine transformation, false otherwise.

symbols.name: reset
symbols.description: Resets the Transform to an identity state. All previously applied transformations are erased.

returns.name: transform
returns.description: The Transform object the method was called on. Allows easily chaining Transform methods.

symbols.name: rotate
symbols.description: Applies a rotation to the Transform's coordinate system. This method does not reset any previously applied transformations.

arguments.name: angle
arguments.description: The relative angle in radians to rotate this Transform by.

returns.name: transform
returns.description: The Transform object the method was called on. Allows easily chaining Transform methods.

symbols.name: scale
symbols.description: Scales the Transform's coordinate system. This method does not reset any previously applied transformations.

arguments.name: sx
arguments.description: The relative scale factor along the x-axis.

arguments.name: sy
arguments.description: The relative scale factor along the y-axis.

returns.name: transform
returns.description: The Transform object the method was called on. Allows easily chaining Transform methods.

Applies the Transform object's transformation to the given 2D position.

arguments.name: globalY

returns.name: localX

returns.name: localY

arguments.name: dy

symbols.name: getCursor
symbols.description: Gets the current Cursor.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The current cursor, or nil if no cursor is set.

symbols.name: getPosition
symbols.description: Returns the current position of the mouse.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The position of the mouse along the x-axis.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The position of the mouse along the y-axis.

symbols.name: getRelativeMode
symbols.description:
Gets whether relative mode is enabled for the mouse.

If relative mode is enabled, the cursor is hidden and doesn't move when the mouse does, but relative mouse motion events are still generated via love.mousemoved. This lets the mouse move in any direction indefinitely without the cursor getting stuck at the edges of the screen.

The reported position of the mouse is not updated while relative mode is enabled, even when relative mouse motion events are generated.

returns.name: enabled
returns.description: True if relative mode is enabled, false if it's disabled.

symbols.name: getSystemCursor
symbols.description:
Gets a Cursor object representing a system-native hardware cursor.

Hardware cursors are framerate-independent and work the same way as normal operating system cursors. Unlike drawing an image at the mouse's current coordinates, hardware cursors never have visible lag between when the mouse is moved and when the cursor position updates, even at low framerates.

arguments.name: ctype
arguments.description: The type of system cursor to get.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The Cursor object representing the system cursor type.

symbols.name: getX
symbols.description: Returns the current x-position of the mouse.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The position of the mouse along the x-axis.

symbols.name: getY
symbols.description: Returns the current y-position of the mouse.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The position of the mouse along the y-axis.

symbols.name: isCursorSupported
symbols.description:
Gets whether cursor functionality is supported.

If it isn't supported, calling love.mouse.newCursor and love.mouse.getSystemCursor will cause an error. Mobile devices do not support cursors.

returns.name: supported
returns.description: Whether the system has cursor functionality.

symbols.name: isDown
symbols.description:
Checks whether a certain mouse button is down.

This function does not detect mouse wheel scrolling; you must use the love.wheelmoved (or love.mousepressed in version 0.9.2 and older) callback for that.

arguments.name: button
arguments.description: The index of a button to check. 1 is the primary mouse button, 2 is the secondary mouse button and 3 is the middle button. Further buttons are mouse dependant.
Additional button numbers to check.

returns.name: down
returns.description: True if any specified button is down.

symbols.name: isGrabbed
symbols.description: Checks if the mouse is grabbed.

returns.name: grabbed
returns.description: True if the cursor is grabbed, false if it is not.

symbols.name: isVisible
symbols.description: Checks if the cursor is visible.

returns.name: visible
returns.description: True if the cursor to visible, false if the cursor is hidden.

symbols.name: newCursor
symbols.description:
Creates a new hardware Cursor object from an image file or ImageData.

Hardware cursors are framerate-independent and work the same way as normal operating system cursors. Unlike drawing an image at the mouse's current coordinates, hardware cursors never have visible lag between when the mouse is moved and when the cursor position updates, even at low framerates.

The hot spot is the point the operating system uses to determine what was clicked and at what position the mouse cursor is. For example, the normal arrow pointer normally has its hot spot at the top left of the image, but a crosshair cursor might have it in the middle.

arguments.name: imageData
arguments.description: The ImageData to use for the new Cursor.

arguments.name: hotx
arguments.description: The x-coordinate in the ImageData of the cursor's hot spot.

arguments.name: hoty
arguments.description: The y-coordinate in the ImageData of the cursor's hot spot.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The new Cursor object.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: Path to the image to use for the new Cursor.

arguments.name: hotx
arguments.description: The x-coordinate in the image of the cursor's hot spot.

arguments.name: hoty
arguments.description: The y-coordinate in the image of the cursor's hot spot.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The new Cursor object.

arguments.name: fileData
arguments.description: Data representing the image to use for the new Cursor.

arguments.name: hotx
arguments.description: The x-coordinate in the image of the cursor's hot spot.

arguments.name: hoty
arguments.description: The y-coordinate in the image of the cursor's hot spot.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The new Cursor object.

symbols.name: setCursor
symbols.description: Sets the current mouse cursor.

arguments.name: cursor
arguments.description: The Cursor object to use as the current mouse cursor.

symbols.name: setGrabbed
symbols.description: Grabs the mouse and confines it to the window.

arguments.name: grab
arguments.description: True to confine the mouse, false to let it leave the window.

symbols.name: setPosition
symbols.description: Sets the current position of the mouse. Non-integer values are floored.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The new position of the mouse along the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The new position of the mouse along the y-axis.

symbols.name: setRelativeMode
symbols.description:
Sets whether relative mode is enabled for the mouse.

When relative mode is enabled, the cursor is hidden and doesn't move when the mouse does, but relative mouse motion events are still generated via love.mousemoved. This lets the mouse move in any direction indefinitely without the cursor getting stuck at the edges of the screen.

The reported position of the mouse may not be updated while relative mode is enabled, even when relative mouse motion events are generated.

arguments.name: enable
arguments.description: True to enable relative mode, false to disable it.

symbols.name: setVisible
symbols.description: Sets the current visibility of the cursor.

arguments.name: visible
arguments.description: True to set the cursor to visible, false to hide the cursor.

symbols.name: setX
symbols.description:
Sets the current X position of the mouse.

Non-integer values are floored.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The new position of the mouse along the x-axis.

symbols.name: setY
symbols.description:
Sets the current Y position of the mouse.

Non-integer values are floored.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The new position of the mouse along the y-axis.

symbols.name: getType
symbols.description: Gets the type of the Cursor.

returns.name: ctype
returns.description: The type of the Cursor.

symbols.name: getDistance
symbols.description: Returns the two closest points between two fixtures and their distance.

arguments.name: fixture1
arguments.description: The first fixture.

arguments.name: fixture2
arguments.description: The second fixture.

returns.name: distance
returns.description: The distance of the two points.

returns.name: x1
returns.description: The x-coordinate of the first point.

returns.name: y1
returns.description: The y-coordinate of the first point.

returns.name: x2
returns.description: The x-coordinate of the second point.

returns.name: y2
returns.description: The y-coordinate of the second point.

symbols.name: getMeter
symbols.description:
Returns the meter scale factor.

All coordinates in the physics module are divided by this number, creating a convenient way to draw the objects directly to the screen without the need for graphics transformations.

It is recommended to create shapes no larger than 10 times the scale. This is important because Box2D is tuned to work well with shape sizes from 0.1 to 10 meters.

returns.name: scale
returns.description: The scale factor as an integer.

symbols.name: newBody
symbols.description:
Creates a new body.

There are three types of bodies.

* Static bodies do not move, have a infinite mass, and can be used for level boundaries.

* Dynamic bodies are the main actors in the simulation, they collide with everything.

* Kinematic bodies do not react to forces and only collide with dynamic bodies.

The mass of the body gets calculated when a Fixture is attached or removed, but can be changed at any time with Body:setMass or Body:resetMassData.

arguments.name: world
arguments.description: The world to create the body in.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the body.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the body.

arguments.name: type
arguments.description: The type of the body.

returns.name: body
returns.description: A new body.

symbols.name: newChainShape
symbols.description: Creates a new ChainShape.

arguments.name: loop
arguments.description: If the chain should loop back to the first point.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the first point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the first point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the second point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the second point.
Additional point positions.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

arguments.name: loop
arguments.description: If the chain should loop back to the first point.

arguments.name: points
arguments.description: A list of points to construct the ChainShape, in the form of {x1, y1, x2, y2, ...}.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

symbols.name: newCircleShape
symbols.description: Creates a new CircleShape.

arguments.name: radius
arguments.description: The radius of the circle.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the circle.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the circle.

arguments.name: radius
arguments.description: The radius of the circle.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

symbols.name: newDistanceJoint
symbols.description:
Creates a DistanceJoint between two bodies.

This joint constrains the distance between two points on two bodies to be constant. These two points are specified in world coordinates and the two bodies are assumed to be in place when this joint is created. The first anchor point is connected to the first body and the second to the second body, and the points define the length of the distance joint.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the first anchor point (world space).

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the first anchor point (world space).

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the second anchor point (world space).

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the second anchor point (world space).

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new distance joint.

symbols.name: newEdgeShape
symbols.description: Creates a new EdgeShape.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the first point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the first point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the second point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the second point.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

symbols.name: newFixture
symbols.description:
Creates and attaches a Fixture to a body.

Note that the Shape object is copied rather than kept as a reference when the Fixture is created. To get the Shape object that the Fixture owns, use Fixture:getShape.

arguments.name: body
arguments.description: The body which gets the fixture attached.

arguments.name: shape
arguments.description: The shape to be copied to the fixture.

arguments.name: density
arguments.description: The density of the fixture.

returns.name: fixture
returns.description: The new fixture.

symbols.name: newFrictionJoint
symbols.description: Create a friction joint between two bodies. A FrictionJoint applies friction to a body.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the anchor point.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the anchor point.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new FrictionJoint.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the first anchor point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the first anchor point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the second anchor point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the second anchor point.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new FrictionJoint.

symbols.name: newGearJoint
symbols.description:
Create a GearJoint connecting two Joints.

The gear joint connects two joints that must be either  prismatic or  revolute joints. Using this joint requires that the joints it uses connect their respective bodies to the ground and have the ground as the first body. When destroying the bodies and joints you must make sure you destroy the gear joint before the other joints.

The gear joint has a ratio the determines how the angular or distance values of the connected joints relate to each other. The formula coordinate1 + ratio * coordinate2 always has a constant value that is set when the gear joint is created.

arguments.name: joint1
arguments.description: The first joint to connect with a gear joint.

arguments.name: joint2
arguments.description: The second joint to connect with a gear joint.

arguments.name: ratio
arguments.description: The gear ratio.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new gear joint.

symbols.name: newMotorJoint
symbols.description:
Creates a joint between two bodies which controls the relative motion between them.

Position and rotation offsets can be specified once the MotorJoint has been created, as well as the maximum motor force and torque that will be be applied to reach the target offsets.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: correctionFactor
arguments.description: The joint's initial position correction factor, in the range of 1.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new MotorJoint.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: correctionFactor
arguments.description: The joint's initial position correction factor, in the range of 1.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new MotorJoint.

symbols.name: newMouseJoint
symbols.description:
Create a joint between a body and the mouse.

This joint actually connects the body to a fixed point in the world. To make it follow the mouse, the fixed point must be updated every timestep (example below).

The advantage of using a MouseJoint instead of just changing a body position directly is that collisions and reactions to other joints are handled by the physics engine.

arguments.name: body
arguments.description: The body to attach to the mouse.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the connecting point.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the connecting point.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new mouse joint.

symbols.name: newPolygonShape
symbols.description:
Creates a new PolygonShape.

This shape can have 8 vertices at most, and must form a convex shape.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the first point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the first point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the second point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the second point.

arguments.name: x3
arguments.description: The x position of the third point.

arguments.name: y3
arguments.description: The y position of the third point.
You can continue passing more point positions to create the PolygonShape.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: A new PolygonShape.

arguments.name: vertices
arguments.description: A list of vertices to construct the polygon, in the form of {x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3, ...}.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: A new PolygonShape.

symbols.name: newPrismaticJoint
symbols.description:
Creates a PrismaticJoint between two bodies.

A prismatic joint constrains two bodies to move relatively to each other on a specified axis. It does not allow for relative rotation. Its definition and operation are similar to a  revolute joint, but with translation and force substituted for angle and torque.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the anchor point.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the anchor point.

arguments.name: ax
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the axis vector.

arguments.name: ay
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the axis vector.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new prismatic joint.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the first anchor point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the first anchor point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the second anchor point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the second anchor point.

arguments.name: ax
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the axis unit vector.

arguments.name: ay
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the axis unit vector.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint

symbols.name: newPulleyJoint
symbols.description:
Creates a PulleyJoint to join two bodies to each other and the ground.

The pulley joint simulates a pulley with an optional block and tackle. If the ratio parameter has a value different from one, then the simulated rope extends faster on one side than the other. In a pulley joint the total length of the simulated rope is the constant length1 + ratio * length2, which is set when the pulley joint is created.

Pulley joints can behave unpredictably if one side is fully extended. It is recommended that the method  setMaxLengths  be used to constrain the maximum lengths each side can attain.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to connect with a pulley joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to connect with a pulley joint.

arguments.name: gx1
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the first body's ground anchor.

arguments.name: gy1
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the first body's ground anchor.

arguments.name: gx2
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the second body's ground anchor.

arguments.name: gy2
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the second body's ground anchor.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the pulley joint anchor in the first body.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the pulley joint anchor in the first body.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the pulley joint anchor in the second body.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the pulley joint anchor in the second body.

arguments.name: ratio
arguments.description: The joint ratio.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new pulley joint.

symbols.name: setMeter
symbols.description:
Sets the pixels to meter scale factor.

All coordinates in the physics module are divided by this number and converted to meters, and it creates a convenient way to draw the objects directly to the screen without the need for graphics transformations.

It is recommended to create shapes no larger than 10 times the scale. This is important because Box2D is tuned to work well with shape sizes from 0.1 to 10 meters. The default meter scale is 30.

arguments.name: scale
arguments.description: The scale factor as an integer.

symbols.name: applyAngularImpulse
symbols.description:
Applies an angular impulse to a body. This makes a single, instantaneous addition to the body momentum.

A body with with a larger mass will react less. The reaction does '''not''' depend on the timestep, and is equivalent to applying a force continuously for 1 second. Impulses are best used to give a single push to a body. For a continuous push to a body it is better to use Body:applyForce.

arguments.name: impulse
arguments.description: The impulse in kilogram-square meter per second.

symbols.name: applyForce
symbols.description:
Apply force to a Body.

A force pushes a body in a direction. A body with with a larger mass will react less. The reaction also depends on how long a force is applied: since the force acts continuously over the entire timestep, a short timestep will only push the body for a short time. Thus forces are best used for many timesteps to give a continuous push to a body (like gravity). For a single push that is independent of timestep, it is better to use Body:applyLinearImpulse.

If the position to apply the force is not given, it will act on the center of mass of the body. The part of the force not directed towards the center of mass will cause the body to spin (and depends on the rotational inertia).

Note that the force components and position must be given in world coordinates.

arguments.name: fx
arguments.description: The x component of force to apply to the center of mass.

arguments.name: fy
arguments.description: The y component of force to apply to the center of mass.

arguments.name: fx
arguments.description: The x component of force to apply.

arguments.name: fy
arguments.description: The y component of force to apply.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position to apply the force.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position to apply the force.

symbols.name: applyLinearImpulse
symbols.description:
Applies an impulse to a body.

This makes a single, instantaneous addition to the body momentum.

An impulse pushes a body in a direction. A body with with a larger mass will react less. The reaction does '''not''' depend on the timestep, and is equivalent to applying a force continuously for 1 second. Impulses are best used to give a single push to a body. For a continuous push to a body it is better to use Body:applyForce.

If the position to apply the impulse is not given, it will act on the center of mass of the body. The part of the impulse not directed towards the center of mass will cause the body to spin (and depends on the rotational inertia).

Note that the impulse components and position must be given in world coordinates.

arguments.name: ix
arguments.description: The x component of the impulse applied to the center of mass.

arguments.name: iy
arguments.description: The y component of the impulse applied to the center of mass.

arguments.name: ix
arguments.description: The x component of the impulse.

arguments.name: iy
arguments.description: The y component of the impulse.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position to apply the impulse.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position to apply the impulse.

symbols.name: applyTorque
symbols.description:
Apply torque to a body.

Torque is like a force that will change the angular velocity (spin) of a body. The effect will depend on the rotational inertia a body has.

arguments.name: torque
arguments.description: The torque to apply.

symbols.name: destroy
symbols.description:
Explicitly destroys the Body and all fixtures and joints attached to it.

An error will occur if you attempt to use the object after calling this function. In 0.7.2, when you don't have time to wait for garbage collection, this function may be used to free the object immediately.

symbols.name: getAngle
symbols.description:
Get the angle of the body.

The angle is measured in radians. If you need to transform it to degrees, use math.deg.

A value of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes ''clockwise'' from our point of view.

returns.name: angle
returns.description: The angle in radians.

symbols.name: getAngularDamping
symbols.description:
Gets the Angular damping of the Body

The angular damping is the ''rate of decrease of the angular velocity over time'': A spinning body with no damping and no external forces will continue spinning indefinitely. A spinning body with damping will gradually stop spinning.

Damping is not the same as friction - they can be modelled together. However, only damping is provided by Box2D (and LOVE).

Damping parameters should be between 0 and infinity, with 0 meaning no damping, and infinity meaning full damping. Normally you will use a damping value between 0 and 0.1.

returns.name: damping
returns.description: The value of the angular damping.

symbols.name: getAngularVelocity
symbols.description:
Get the angular velocity of the Body.

The angular velocity is the ''rate of change of angle over time''.

It is changed in World:update by applying torques, off centre forces/impulses, and angular damping. It can be set directly with Body:setAngularVelocity.

If you need the ''rate of change of position over time'', use Body:getLinearVelocity.

returns.name: w
returns.description: The angular velocity in radians/second.

symbols.name: getContacts
symbols.description: Gets a list of all Contacts attached to the Body.

returns.name: contacts
returns.description: A list with all contacts associated with the Body.

symbols.name: getFixtures
symbols.description: Returns a table with all fixtures.

returns.name: fixtures
returns.description: A sequence with all fixtures.

symbols.name: getGravityScale
symbols.description: Returns the gravity scale factor.

returns.name: scale
returns.description: The gravity scale factor.

symbols.name: getInertia
symbols.description:
Gets the rotational inertia of the body.

The rotational inertia is how hard is it to make the body spin.

returns.name: inertia
returns.description: The rotational inertial of the body.

symbols.name: getJoints
symbols.description: Returns a table containing the Joints attached to this Body.

returns.name: joints
returns.description: A sequence with the Joints attached to the Body.

symbols.name: getLinearDamping
symbols.description:
Gets the linear damping of the Body.

The linear damping is the ''rate of decrease of the linear velocity over time''. A moving body with no damping and no external forces will continue moving indefinitely, as is the case in space. A moving body with damping will gradually stop moving.

Damping is not the same as friction - they can be modelled together.

returns.name: damping
returns.description: The value of the linear damping.

symbols.name: getLinearVelocity
symbols.description:
Gets the linear velocity of the Body from its center of mass.

The linear velocity is the ''rate of change of position over time''.

If you need the ''rate of change of angle over time'', use Body:getAngularVelocity.

If you need to get the linear velocity of a point different from the center of mass:

*  Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint allows you to specify the point in local coordinates.

*  Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint allows you to specify the point in world coordinates.

See page 136 of 'Essential Mathematics for Games and Interactive Applications' for definitions of local and world coordinates.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-component of the velocity vector

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-component of the velocity vector

symbols.name: getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint
symbols.description:
Get the linear velocity of a point on the body.

The linear velocity for a point on the body is the velocity of the body center of mass plus the velocity at that point from the body spinning.

The point on the body must given in local coordinates. Use Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint to specify this with world coordinates.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position to measure velocity.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position to measure velocity.

returns.name: vx
returns.description: The x component of velocity at point (x,y).

returns.name: vy
returns.description: The y component of velocity at point (x,y).

symbols.name: getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint
symbols.description:
Get the linear velocity of a point on the body.

The linear velocity for a point on the body is the velocity of the body center of mass plus the velocity at that point from the body spinning.

The point on the body must given in world coordinates. Use Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint to specify this with local coordinates.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position to measure velocity.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position to measure velocity.

returns.name: vx
returns.description: The x component of velocity at point (x,y).

returns.name: vy

symbols.name: isActive
symbols.description: Returns whether the body is actively used in the simulation.

returns.name: status
returns.description: True if the body is active or false if not.

symbols.name: isAwake
symbols.description: Returns the sleep status of the body.

returns.name: status
returns.description: True if the body is awake or false if not.

symbols.name: isBullet
symbols.description:
Get the bullet status of a body.

There are two methods to check for body collisions:

*  at their location when the world is updated (default)

*  using continuous collision detection (CCD)

The default method is efficient, but a body moving very quickly may sometimes jump over another body without producing a collision. A body that is set as a bullet will use CCD. This is less efficient, but is guaranteed not to jump when moving quickly.

Note that static bodies (with zero mass) always use CCD, so your walls will not let a fast moving body pass through even if it is not a bullet.

returns.name: status
returns.description: The bullet status of the body.

symbols.name: isDestroyed
symbols.description: Gets whether the Body is destroyed. Destroyed bodies cannot be used.

returns.name: destroyed
returns.description: Whether the Body is destroyed.

symbols.name: isFixedRotation
symbols.description: Returns whether the body rotation is locked.

returns.name: fixed
returns.description: True if the body's rotation is locked or false if not.

symbols.name: isSleepingAllowed
symbols.description: Returns the sleeping behaviour of the body.

returns.name: allowed
returns.description: True if the body is allowed to sleep or false if not.

symbols.name: isTouching
symbols.description: Gets whether the Body is touching the given other Body.

arguments.name: otherbody
arguments.description: The other body to check.

returns.name: touching
returns.description: True if this body is touching the other body, false otherwise.

symbols.name: resetMassData
symbols.description: Resets the mass of the body by recalculating it from the mass properties of the fixtures.

symbols.name: setActive
symbols.description:
Sets whether the body is active in the world.

An inactive body does not take part in the simulation. It will not move or cause any collisions.

arguments.name: active
arguments.description: If the body is active or not.

symbols.name: setAngle
symbols.description:
Set the angle of the body.

The angle is measured in radians. If you need to transform it from degrees, use math.rad.

A value of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes ''clockwise'' from our point of view.

It is possible to cause a collision with another body by changing its angle.

arguments.name: angle
arguments.description: The angle in radians.

symbols.name: setAngularDamping
symbols.description:
Sets the angular damping of a Body

See Body:getAngularDamping for a definition of angular damping.

Angular damping can take any value from 0 to infinity. It is recommended to stay between 0 and 0.1, though. Other values will look unrealistic.

arguments.name: damping
arguments.description: The new angular damping.

symbols.name: setAngularVelocity
symbols.description:
Sets the angular velocity of a Body.

The angular velocity is the ''rate of change of angle over time''.

This function will not accumulate anything; any impulses previously applied since the last call to World:update will be lost.

arguments.name: w
arguments.description: The new angular velocity, in radians per second

symbols.name: setAwake
symbols.description: Wakes the body up or puts it to sleep.

arguments.name: awake
arguments.description: The body sleep status.

symbols.name: setBullet
symbols.description:
Set the bullet status of a body.

There are two methods to check for body collisions:

*  at their location when the world is updated (default)

*  using continuous collision detection (CCD)

The default method is efficient, but a body moving very quickly may sometimes jump over another body without producing a collision. A body that is set as a bullet will use CCD. This is less efficient, but is guaranteed not to jump when moving quickly.

Note that static bodies (with zero mass) always use CCD, so your walls will not let a fast moving body pass through even if it is not a bullet.

arguments.name: status
arguments.description: The bullet status of the body.

symbols.name: setFixedRotation
symbols.description:
Set whether a body has fixed rotation.

Bodies with fixed rotation don't vary the speed at which they rotate. Calling this function causes the mass to be reset.

arguments.name: isFixed

symbols.name: setSleepingAllowed
symbols.description: Sets the sleeping behaviour of the body. Should sleeping be allowed, a body at rest will automatically sleep. A sleeping body is not simulated unless it collided with an awake body. Be wary that one can end up with a situation like a floating sleeping body if the floor was removed.

arguments.name: allowed
arguments.description: True if the body is allowed to sleep or false if not.

symbols.name: setTransform
symbols.description:
Set the position and angle of the body.

Note that the position may not be the center of mass of the body. An angle of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes clockwise from our point of view.

This function cannot wake up the body.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x component of the position.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y component of the position.

arguments.name: angle
arguments.description: The angle in radians.

symbols.name: rayCast
symbols.description:
Casts a ray against the shape of the fixture and returns the surface normal vector and the line position where the ray hit. If the ray missed the shape, nil will be returned.

The ray starts on the first point of the input line and goes towards the second point of the line. The fifth argument is the maximum distance the ray is going to travel as a scale factor of the input line length.

The childIndex parameter is used to specify which child of a parent shape, such as a ChainShape, will be ray casted. For ChainShapes, the index of 1 is the first edge on the chain. Ray casting a parent shape will only test the child specified so if you want to test every shape of the parent, you must loop through all of its children.

The world position of the impact can be calculated by multiplying the line vector with the third return value and adding it to the line starting point.

hitx, hity = x1 + (x2 - x1) * fraction, y1 + (y2 - y1) * fraction

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the input line starting point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the input line starting point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the input line end point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the input line end point.

arguments.name: maxFraction
arguments.description: Ray length parameter.

arguments.name: childIndex
arguments.description: The index of the child the ray gets cast against.

returns.name: xn
returns.description: The x component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit the shape.

returns.name: yn
returns.description: The y component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit the shape.

returns.name: fraction
returns.description: The position on the input line where the intersection happened as a factor of the line length.

symbols.name: setFilterData
symbols.description:
Sets the filter data of the fixture.

Groups, categories, and mask can be used to define the collision behaviour of the fixture.

If two fixtures are in the same group they either always collide if the group is positive, or never collide if it's negative. If the group is zero or they do not match, then the contact filter checks if the fixtures select a category of the other fixture with their masks. The fixtures do not collide if that's not the case. If they do have each other's categories selected, the return value of the custom contact filter will be used. They always collide if none was set.

There can be up to 16 categories. Categories and masks are encoded as the bits of a 16-bit integer.

When created, prior to calling this function, all fixtures have category set to 1, mask set to 65535 (all categories) and group set to 0.

This function allows setting all filter data for a fixture at once. To set only the categories, the mask or the group, you can use Fixture:setCategory, Fixture:setMask or Fixture:setGroupIndex respectively.

arguments.name: categories
arguments.description: The categories as an integer from 0 to 65535.

arguments.name: mask
arguments.description: The mask as an integer from 0 to 65535.

arguments.name: group
arguments.description: The group as an integer from -32768 to 32767.

symbols.name: setFriction
symbols.description:
Sets the friction of the fixture.

Friction determines how shapes react when they 'slide' along other shapes. Low friction indicates a slippery surface, like ice, while high friction indicates a rough surface, like concrete. Range: 0.0 - 1.0.

arguments.name: friction
arguments.description: The fixture friction.

symbols.name: setGroupIndex
symbols.description:
Sets the group the fixture belongs to. Fixtures with the same group will always collide if the group is positive or never collide if it's negative. The group zero means no group.

The groups range from -32768 to 32767.

arguments.name: group
arguments.description: The group as an integer from -32768 to 32767.

symbols.name: setMask
symbols.description:
Sets the category mask of the fixture. There can be up to 16 categories represented as a number from 1 to 16.

This fixture will '''NOT''' collide with the fixtures that are in the selected categories if the other fixture also has a category of this fixture selected.

arguments.description: The masks.

symbols.name: setSensor

Sets whether the fixture should act as a sensor.

Sensors do not cause collision responses, but the begin-contact and end-contact World callbacks will still be called for this fixture.

arguments.name: sensor

symbols.name: rayCast
symbols.description:
Casts a ray against the shape and returns the surface normal vector and the line position where the ray hit. If the ray missed the shape, nil will be returned. The Shape can be transformed to get it into the desired position.

The ray starts on the first point of the input line and goes towards the second point of the line. The fourth argument is the maximum distance the ray is going to travel as a scale factor of the input line length.

The childIndex parameter is used to specify which child of a parent shape, such as a ChainShape, will be ray casted. For ChainShapes, the index of 1 is the first edge on the chain. Ray casting a parent shape will only test the child specified so if you want to test every shape of the parent, you must loop through all of its children.

The world position of the impact can be calculated by multiplying the line vector with the third return value and adding it to the line starting point.

hitx, hity = x1 + (x2 - x1) * fraction, y1 + (y2 - y1) * fraction

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the input line starting point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the input line starting point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the input line end point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the input line end point.

arguments.name: maxFraction
arguments.description: Ray length parameter.

arguments.name: tx
arguments.description: The translation of the shape on the x-axis.

arguments.name: ty
arguments.description: The translation of the shape on the y-axis.

arguments.name: tr
arguments.description: The shape rotation.

arguments.name: childIndex
arguments.description: The index of the child the ray gets cast against.

returns.name: xn
returns.description: The x component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit the shape.

returns.name: yn
returns.description: The y component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit the shape.

returns.name: fraction
returns.description: The position on the input line where the intersection happened as a factor of the line length.

symbols.name: testPoint
symbols.description: This is particularly useful for mouse interaction with the shapes. By looping through all shapes and testing the mouse position with this function, we can find which shapes the mouse touches.

arguments.name: tx
arguments.description: Translates the shape along the x-axis.

arguments.name: ty
arguments.description: Translates the shape along the y-axis.

arguments.name: tr
arguments.description: Rotates the shape.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-component of the point.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-component of the point.

returns.name: hit
returns.description: True if inside, false if outside

symbols.name: queryBoundingBox
symbols.description: Calls a function for each fixture inside the specified area by searching for any overlapping bounding box (Fixture:getBoundingBox).

arguments.name: topLeftX
arguments.description: The x position of the top-left point.

arguments.name: topLeftY
arguments.description: The y position of the top-left point.

arguments.name: bottomRightX
arguments.description: The x position of the bottom-right point.

arguments.name: bottomRightY
arguments.description: The y position of the bottom-right point.

arguments.name: callback
arguments.description: This function gets passed one argument, the fixture, and should return a boolean. The search will continue if it is true or stop if it is false.

symbols.name: rayCast
symbols.description: Casts a ray and calls a function for each fixtures it intersects.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the starting point of the ray.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The x position of the starting point of the ray.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the end point of the ray.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The x value of the surface normal vector of the shape edge.

arguments.name: callback
arguments.description: A function called for each fixture intersected by the ray. The function gets six arguments and should return a number as a control value. The intersection points fed into the function will be in an arbitrary order. If you wish to find the closest point of intersection, you'll need to do that yourself within the function. The easiest way to do that is by using the fraction value.

symbols.name: setCallbacks
symbols.description:
Sets functions for the collision callbacks during the world update.

Four Lua functions can be given as arguments. The value nil removes a function.

When called, each function will be passed three arguments. The first two arguments are the colliding fixtures and the third argument is the Contact between them. The postSolve callback additionally gets the normal and tangent impulse for each contact point. See notes.

If you are interested to know when exactly each callback is called, consult a Box2d manual

arguments.name: beginContact
arguments.description: Gets called when two fixtures begin to overlap.

arguments.name: endContact
arguments.description: Gets called when two fixtures cease to overlap. This will also be called outside of a world update, when colliding objects are destroyed.

arguments.name: preSolve
arguments.description: Gets called before a collision gets resolved.

arguments.name: postSolve
arguments.description: Gets called after the collision has been resolved.

symbols.name: setContactFilter
symbols.description:
Sets a function for collision filtering.

If the group and category filtering doesn't generate a collision decision, this function gets called with the two fixtures as arguments. The function should return a boolean value where true means the fixtures will collide and false means they will pass through each other.

arguments.name: filter
arguments.description: The function handling the contact filtering.

arguments.description: The x component of gravity.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y component of gravity.

symbols.name: setSleepingAllowed
symbols.description: Sets the sleep behaviour of the world.

arguments.name: allow

symbols.name: newSoundData
symbols.description:
Creates new SoundData from a filepath, File, or Decoder. It's also possible to create SoundData with a custom sample rate, channel and bit depth.

The sound data will be decoded to the memory in a raw format. It is recommended to create only short sounds like effects, as a 3 minute song uses 30 MB of memory this way.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The file name of the file to load.

returns.name: soundData
returns.description: A new SoundData object.

arguments.name: file
arguments.description: A File pointing to an audio file.

returns.name: soundData
returns.description: A new SoundData object.

arguments.name: decoder
arguments.description: Decode data from this Decoder until EOF.

returns.name: soundData
returns.description: A new SoundData object.

arguments.name: samples
arguments.description: Total number of samples.

arguments.name: rate
arguments.description: Number of samples per second

arguments.name: bits
arguments.description: Bits per sample (8 or 16).

arguments.name: channels
arguments.description: Either 1 for mono or 2 for stereo.

returns.name: soundData
returns.description: A new SoundData object.

The new decoder will start decoding from the beginning of the audio stream.
